name: Rate Limiter
overview:
A rate limiter controls the rate of requests from clients to protect backend services from overload and abuse. It
must enforce per-user, per-IP, and per-API-key rate limits with sub-millisecond overhead, support multiple rate
limiting algorithms (token bucket, sliding window), work correctly in a distributed environment (multiple API
gateway instances), and provide informative error responses with retry-after headers.

inScope: Enforce rate limits per user, IP, and API key
Support token bucket and sliding window algorithms
Distributed: consistent limits across multiple gateway nodes
Return 429 with Retry-After header on limit exceeded

items: Support 1M requests/second across all clients with <1ms overhead per rate limit check
Distributed: limits enforced consistently across all gateway nodes — not multiplied per node
99.99% availability; Redis failure must fall back gracefully (local counters) rather than blocking all traffic
Rate limit rules propagate to all gateways within 60 seconds of update
Informative 429 responses with Retry-After and X-RateLimit headers for client backoff
Horizontally scalable: adding gateway nodes must not change effective rate limits

entities.name: RateLimit
entities.schema:
{
  limit_id: string,  // e.g., 'user:123:api_calls'
  algorithm: enum(TOKEN_BUCKET|SLIDING_WINDOW|FIXED_WINDOW),
  max_requests: int,
  window_seconds: int,
  current_count: int,
  last_refill: timestamp
}

approaches.title: Fixed Window Counter
approaches.description: Count requests in fixed time windows (e.g., 100 requests per minute). Reset counter at window boundary. Boundary problem: a client can make 100 requests at 11:59 and 100 more at 12:00, effectively making 200 requests in 2 seconds.
approaches.tradeoff: Boundary burst allows 2x the limit at window boundaries.

approaches.title: Sliding Window Log
approaches.description: Store timestamp of each request in a sorted set. Count requests in the last N seconds. Accurate but memory-intensive: stores one entry per request. At 1M req/sec, this requires enormous Redis memory.
approaches.tradeoff: High memory usage; O(N) per request for cleanup.

approaches.title: Sliding Window Counter (Hybrid)
approaches.description: Combine fixed window counters with interpolation. Keep counters for current and previous windows. Estimate current rate as: prev_count × (1 - elapsed/window) + curr_count. Memory-efficient (only 2 counters per client) and approximately accurate (within 0.003% error for most traffic patterns). Implemented as a Redis Lua script for atomicity.
approaches.tradeoff: Approximate (not exact) sliding window; error is negligible for most use cases.

approaches.title: Local In-Memory Counter
approaches.description: Each API gateway node maintains its own in-memory counter. With 10 gateway nodes, each node allows 100 requests/minute, giving a total of 1000 requests/minute — 10x the intended limit.
approaches.tradeoff: Limit is multiplied by number of nodes; not a true distributed limit.

approaches.title: Centralized Redis Counter
approaches.description: All gateway nodes share a single Redis instance for rate limit counters. Correct but adds a Redis round-trip to every request (~1ms). At 1M req/sec, Redis becomes a bottleneck.
approaches.tradeoff: Redis round-trip adds latency; Redis is a single point of failure.

approaches.title: Local Counter + Periodic Sync
approaches.description: Each gateway node maintains a local counter. Every 100ms, nodes sync their local counts to Redis and fetch the global count. Requests are allowed if local_count + global_count < limit. This allows slight over-counting (up to 100ms × rate) but eliminates per-request Redis calls. For strict limits, use synchronous Redis Lua script. For approximate limits, use async sync.
approaches.tradeoff: Slight over-counting during sync interval; not suitable for strict financial rate limits.

components.name: API Gateway
components.responsibility: Intercepts all requests; calls Rate Limiter middleware; forwards allowed requests

components.name: Rate Limiter Middleware
components.responsibility: Checks rate limits; returns 429 if exceeded; adds rate limit headers

components.name: Redis Cluster
components.responsibility: Stores rate limit counters; Lua scripts for atomic check-and-increment

components.name: Rate Limit Config DB
components.responsibility: Stores rate limit rules per user/IP/API key; cached in gateway memory

components.name: Admin API
components.responsibility: Manages rate limit rules; supports dynamic updates

components.name: Metrics Service
components.responsibility: Tracks rate limit hit rates per client; identifies abusive clients

questions.title: 2. Sliding Window Counter Implementation
questions.content:
The sliding window counter uses two Redis keys per client: current_window_count and
previous_window_count. On each request: (1) Compute elapsed = (now % window_seconds) /
window_seconds, (2) Estimate = prev_count × (1 - elapsed) + curr_count, (3) If estimate < limit, increment
curr_count and allow. At window boundary, curr_count becomes prev_count and curr_count resets to 0.

questions.title: 3. Rate Limit Headers
questions.content:
The rate limiter adds informative headers to every response: X-RateLimit-Limit (the limit),
X-RateLimit-Remaining (remaining requests in current window), X-RateLimit-Reset (Unix timestamp when the
window resets), Retry-After (seconds until the client can retry, only on 429). These headers allow clients to
implement adaptive request rates and avoid hammering the rate limiter.

questions.title: 4. Handling Redis Failures
questions.content:
If Redis is unavailable, the rate limiter falls back to local in-memory counters. This allows slight over-counting
but prevents a Redis outage from taking down the entire API. The fallback is time-limited: if Redis is unavailable
for >30 seconds, the rate limiter switches to 'fail open' mode (allow all requests) to prevent service degradation.
Alerts are triggered immediately on Redis failure.

questions.title: 5. Rate Limit Rule Management
questions.content:
Rate limit rules are stored in a configuration DB (PostgreSQL) and cached in each gateway node's memory
(refreshed every 60 seconds). Rules can be defined at multiple levels: global (all users), per-plan (free/paid),
per-user, per-IP. The most specific rule takes precedence. Dynamic rule updates (e.g., blocking an abusive IP)
propagate to all gateways within 60 seconds via cache refresh.

Check · Count · Allow · Reject

API Gateway

Rate Limiter

Redis Cluster
counters + Lua
Backend Svc
protected API
Config DB
rate limit rules
Admin API

HTTP request
check limit
INCR / Lua
allow (200)
reject (429)
rules cache
update rules
keyFlows: ① Client request → API Gateway → Rate Limiter middleware
② Rate Limiter: check Redis counter (Lua script, atomic)
③ Under limit: increment counter → forward to Backend (200)
④ Over limit: return 429 with Retry-After + X-RateLimit headers
⑤ Redis failure: fallback to local in-memory counter (fail open)

whatToStudyFurther.content:
Expert Note: Study all four rate limiting algorithms in depth: fixed window, sliding window log, sliding
window counter, and token bucket — understand the trade-offs between accuracy, memory, and
complexity.
Expert Note: Learn about Redis Lua scripting for atomic operations: why Lua scripts are atomic in Redis
(single-threaded execution), and how to implement complex rate limiting logic without race conditions.
Expert Note: Understand the Leaky Bucket algorithm: how it differs from token bucket, why it's used for
traffic shaping (smooth output rate) vs. rate limiting (burst allowance).
Expert Note: Study distributed rate limiting patterns: the trade-off between consistency (centralized Redis)
and performance (local counters with sync), and how to choose based on use case requirements.
Expert Note: Learn about HTTP rate limit headers: the IETF draft standard for RateLimit headers, how
Retry-After works, and how clients should implement exponential backoff.
